items.q: What were the Wars of the Roses really about?
items.correct: Dynastic conflict between rival branches of the Plantagenets (Lancaster and York) over the throne, intensified by weak kingship under Henry VI and noble factionalism.
items.misconception: The whole country was at war for thirty continuous years.
items.random_wrong: They were a religious war between Catholics and Protestants.
items.why: Fighting was sporadic and most of England saw no battles; ordinary life carried on. Students imagine total civil war; in reality, it was an aristocratic dispute fought in short campaigns.

items.q: Why were the Whitechapel murders never solved?
items.correct: Lack of forensic techniques, no fingerprinting or DNA, conflicting witness accounts, jurisdictional confusion between forces, and possibly police incompetence and media interference.
items.misconception: The police failed to solve the murders because they didn't try hard enough.
items.random_wrong: The Ripper was caught but his name was kept secret to protect the Royal Family.
items.why: The police investigated heavily but lacked tools we take for granted. Royal conspiracy theories are unsupported; the limitation was technological and institutional.

items.q: Why did William of Normandy claim the English throne in 1066?
items.correct: He claimed Edward the Confessor had promised him the throne in 1051 and that Harold Godwinson had sworn an oath to support that claim in 1064.
items.misconception: William invaded because he was the strongest king in Europe and wanted more land.
items.random_wrong: William was Edward the Confessor's eldest son.
items.why: Students treat the invasion as raw conquest, missing that William framed it as enforcing a legitimate hereditary and oath-based claim — legitimacy mattered hugely in 11th-century politics.

items.q: In what year was the Domesday Book completed?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1086. “1066” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What was the name of William's brutal northern campaign?
items.why: It's Harrying of the North. “The Anarchy” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was the Battle of Hastings fought?
items.why: The real figure is 1066 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
